content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 3,
1992
Germina D.
Demers
1009 Coronado Ave.
Spring Valley,
CA
91977
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our rile No.
A-92—586
Dear Ms.
Demers:
This letter is in response to your request for confirmation
and confirms the telephone advice provided to you on October 22,
1992, regarding the “personal use” provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
1.
May a successful candidate use campaign funds to host an
election victory celebration after the elections?
2.
May a candidate use campaign funds to host a party for
the purpose of thanking committee workers and supporters on the
night of the election?
3.
May a defeated candidate use campaign funds to host a
party for the purpose of thanking committee workers and supporters
on the night of the election?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
The committee may use campaign funds for an election
victory celebration to be held after the elections.
2 and 3.
Since a “thank you” event for campaign workers and
supporters to be held on the night of the elections is essentially
an election celebration or similar campaign event,
committee
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-92--586
Page
2
campaign funds may be used to host such an event,
whether the
candidate is successful or unsuccessful.
FACTS
You are currently the treasurer of the Michael Harrelson for
School Board Committee.
The committee would like to hold a party
on the night of the election to thank campaign workers and active
supporters of the campaign.
In the event the candidate is successful,
the committee would
also like to host a victory party after the elections.
2
ANALYSIS
Effective January 1,
1990, the Act was amended to include new
provisions which regulate the appropriate use of campaign funds.
(Section 89510,
g.)
The general rule of the new personal use
law is that any expenditure of campaign funds by a candidate must,
at a minimum,
be reasonably related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose associated with the candidate’s office.
(Section 89512.)
However,
Section 89512.5(b) requires that expenditures by a
committee which confer a substantial personal benefit on any
individual or individuals with authority to approve the
expenditure of campaign funds held by the committee must be
directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental
purpose of the committee.
3
Section 89513 provides a list of specific activities for
which campaign funds may be used.
Under Section 89513(f) (2),
“ta]n election victory celebration or similar
campaign event,
or gifts with a total cumulative
value of less than two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
in a single year made to an individual employee,
a
committee worker,
or an employee of the elected
officer’s agency,
are considered to be directly
related to a political,
legislative,
or
governmental purpose.
Thus,
as you were advised,
campaign funds used for an
election victory celebration or similar campaign event are
statutorily deemed to be directly related to a political,
legislative or governmental purpose.
Accordingly,
the committee
2
These facts contain information you provided to me in the
course of our telephone conversation of October 27,
1992.
A “substantial personal benefit” is defined as a direct
personal benefit to the candidate of $100 or more.
(Section
89511(b) (2).)


Our File No.
A-92-586
Page
3
may use campaign funds to host a victory party on the night of the
elections.
We turn now to the “thank you” event which would be held on
the night of the elections.
Its purpose is to “thank” campaign
workers and supporters of the candidate.
Because of these
characteristics,
the “thank you” event is in the nature of an
election victory celebration or similar campaign event.
(Murphy
Advice Letter,
No.
A-92-116.)
Consequently,
the event would fall
under the permissible use of campaign funds provision found in
Section 895l3(f)(2).
As a result, it would be permissible in this
case to use campaign funds for a “thank you” event regardless of
whether the candidate is successful or unsuccessful.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do
not hesitate to call
me at
(916)
322-59Ol.
Sincerely-,
Scott liallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
B anca
M.
Breeze
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:BMB:dd
“
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of
1Oc per page.
